title: About
text:
The primary source for this dashboard is the IAFF Presumptive Health database, maintained by the International Association of Fire Fighters (iaff.org/presumptive-health). The IAFF tracks presumptive workers’ compensation laws for first responders across all 50 states and publishes a regularly updated summary page for each state, including statute citations, covered conditions, and links to the underlying law.
This dashboard is essentially a structured, map-based interface for the information the IAFF has already compiled. We have not independently verified the legal text of each statute. Users who need authoritative information should consult the IAFF source pages directly — each state entry in the data table links to the corresponding IAFF page.
Two published academic reviews were used to cross-check the initial data extraction and fill gaps not covered by the IAFF pages:

Neugent, K., & Spidell, B. (2023). Firefighters and first responders: 2023 update on presumptive workers comp benefits. National Council on Compensation Insurance (NCCI). Covers approximately 38 NCCI-jurisdiction states; data current through November 2022.
Brandt-Rauf, S., Davis, A. L., & Taylor, J. A. (2024). Inventory of state workers’ compensation laws in the United States: first responder mental health. Journal of Public Health Policy, 45, 562–574. 50-state inventory using the Westlaw legal database; data current through December 2022.

title: About
text:

The IAFF tracks presumptive workers’ compensation laws for first responders across all 50 states and publishes a regularly updated summary page for each state, including statute citations, covered conditions, and links to the underlying law.
This dashboard is essentially a structured, map-based interface for the information the IAFF has already compiled. We have not independently verified the legal text of each statute. Two published academic reviews were used to cross-check the initial data extraction and fill gaps not covered by the IAFF pages:

Neugent, K., & Spidell, B. (2023). Firefighters and first responders: 2023 update on presumptive workers comp benefits. National Council on Compensation Insurance (NCCI). Covers approximately 38 NCCI-jurisdiction states; data current through November 2022.
Brandt-Rauf, S., Davis, A. L., & Taylor, J. A. (2024). Inventory of state workers’ compensation laws in the United States: first responder mental health. Journal of Public Health Policy, 45, 562–574. 50-state inventory using the Westlaw legal database; data current through December 2022.

title: About
text:
Methodology
For each state, IAFF source pages were reviewed and law attributes — condition covered, occupations covered, statute citation, service requirements, post-employment coverage, and related restrictions — were coded into a structured dataset. Each row represents one state × condition × occupation combination.
Where the IAFF source characterized a provision as historical or inactive, that status is reflected in the data. A small number of entries are flagged for independent verification where the active status of a statute is uncertain (marked with * in the Notes column of the data table).
Data currency: The IAFF database is actively maintained, but this dashboard reflects a point-in-time extraction. Laws change. Always verify current status with a qualified attorney or your state’s workers’ compensation board before relying on this resource for legal or financial decisions.

title: About
text:
About ALERRT
This resource was developed by the ALERRT Center at Texas State University. ALERRT conducts research on first responder training, health, and wellness.

title: About
text:
Suggested citation
Tanksley, P. T. (year). First Responder Presumptive Laws Dashboard. ALERRT Center, Texas State University. [URL]

title: First Responder Presumptive Laws
text:
When a first responder is diagnosed with a serious illness — cancer, heart disease, PTSD — they typically must prove that their job caused it to receive workers’ compensation benefits. Presumptive laws shift that burden. Under a presumptive law, the illness is presumed to be work-related, and the employer must prove otherwise.
Coverage varies widely across the country: which conditions are covered, which responders are eligible, and how strong the protections are all differ by state. Keeping track of where things stand — and explaining it in plain terms — is harder than it should be.

title: First Responder Presumptive Laws

title: First Responder Presumptive Laws
text:
About this app
This is a prototype interactive dashboard developed by the ALERRT Center at Texas State University. Our goal is to make information about first responder presumptive laws more accessible to the people who need it most: first responders, their families, union representatives, and policymakers trying to understand where their state stands relative to others.
The underlying data come primarily from the IAFF Presumptive Health database, which is maintained by the International Association of Fire Fighters and covers all 50 states. We’ve structured and mapped that information to make it easier to search and compare — but the IAFF pages are the authoritative source, and we link to them throughout the app.

title: First Responder Presumptive Laws
text:
About ALERRT
The Advanced Law Enforcement Rapid Response Training (ALERRT) Center at Texas State University is a university-based research and training center focused on first responder readiness, training effectiveness, and health and wellness. ALERRT conducts applied research aimed at improving outcomes for first responders and the communities they serve.

title: First Responder Presumptive Laws
text:
How to use this app
Navigate to the Dashboard to explore the data.

Click a state on the map to see all conditions covered in that state and which occupations are protected.
Use the Occupation mode to ask: “Which conditions are covered for firefighters — and which states have the most comprehensive coverage?”
Use the Condition mode to ask: “Which states cover mental health / PTSD — and for which occupations?”
The table below the map shows the details for whatever is selected: statute citations, service requirements, post-employment coverage, and notes.
Hover over bars in the chart to highlight corresponding states on the map. Click a bar to select those states.

This is a prototype. If you notice an error or a law that has changed, see the About page for source information and methodology.
